question: B-4. An automobile starts from rest and ends at rest, traversing a distance of one mile in one minute, along a straight road. If a governor prevents the speed of the car from exceeding ninety miles per hour, show that at some time of the traverse the acceleration or deceleration of the car was at least \( 6.6 \mathrm{ft} . / \mathrm{sec} .^{2} \)
solution:
B-4 Converting units to feet and seconds, we have \( 0 \leqq v(t) \leqq 132 \) for all \( t \in[0,60] \). Suppose \( \left|v^{\prime}(t)\right|<6.6 \) for all \( t \in[0,60] \). Then \( v(t)=\int_{0}^{t} v^{\prime}<6.6 t \), and \( v(t)=\int_{t}^{60}-v^{\prime}<6.6(60-t) \) for all \( t \in[0,60] \). Thus
\[
5280=\int_{0}^{60} v(t) d t<\int_{0}^{60} \min \{6.6 t, 6.6(60-t), 132\} d t .
\]

This last integral is the area under a trapezoid and equals the value 5280 , which is a contradiction.

descriptive_long.question: B-4. An automobile starts from rest and ends at rest, traversing a distance of one mile in one minute, along a straight road. If a governor prevents the speed of the car from exceeding ninety miles per hour, show that at some time of the traverse the acceleration or deceleration of the car was at least \( 6.6 \mathrm{ft} . / \mathrm{sec} .^{2} \)
descriptive_long.solution:
B-4 Converting units to feet and seconds, we have \( 0 \leqq velocity(timevar) \leqq 132 \) for all \( timevar \in[0,60] \). Suppose \( \left|velocity^{\prime}(timevar)\right|<6.6 \) for all \( timevar \in[0,60] \). Then \( velocity(timevar)=\int_{0}^{timevar} velocity^{\prime}<6.6 timevar \), and \( velocity(timevar)=\int_{timevar}^{60}-velocity^{\prime}<6.6(60-timevar) \) for all \( timevar \in[0,60] \). Thus
\[
5280=\int_{0}^{60} velocity(timevar) d timevar<\int_{0}^{60} \min \{6.6 timevar, 6.6(60-timevar), 132\} d timevar .
\]

This last integral is the area under a trapezoid and equals the value 5280 , which is a contradiction.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: B-4. An automobile starts from rest and ends at rest, traversing a distance of one mile in one minute, along a straight road. If a governor prevents the speed of the car from exceeding ninety miles per hour, show that at some time of the traverse the acceleration or deceleration of the car was at least \( 6.6 \mathrm{ft} . / \mathrm{sec} .^{2} \)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-4 Converting units to feet and seconds, we have \( 0 \leqq turnstile(marigold) \leqq 132 \) for all \( marigold \in[0,60] \). Suppose \( \left| turnstile^{\prime}(marigold) \right|<6.6 \) for all \( marigold \in[0,60] \). Then \( turnstile(marigold)=\int_{0}^{marigold} turnstile^{\prime}<6.6 marigold \), and \( turnstile(marigold)=\int_{marigold}^{60}-turnstile^{\prime}<6.6(60-marigold) \) for all \( marigold \in[0,60] \). Thus
\[
5280=\int_{0}^{60} turnstile(marigold) d marigold<\int_{0}^{60} \min \{6.6 marigold, 6.6(60-marigold), 132\} d marigold .
\]

This last integral is the area under a trapezoid and equals the value 5280 , which is a contradiction.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: B-4. An automobile starts from rest and ends at rest, traversing a distance of one mile in one minute, along a straight road. If a governor prevents the speed of the car from exceeding ninety miles per hour, show that at some time of the traverse the acceleration or deceleration of the car was at least \( 6.6 \mathrm{ft} . / \mathrm{sec} .^{2} \)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-4 Converting units to feet and seconds, we have \( 0 \leqq immobility(timelessness) \leqq 132 \) for all \( timelessness \in[0,60] \). Suppose \( \left|immobility^{\prime}(timelessness)\right|<6.6 \) for all \( timelessness \in[0,60] \). Then \( immobility(timelessness)=\int_{0}^{timelessness} immobility^{\prime}<6.6\, timelessness \), and \( immobility(timelessness)=\int_{timelessness}^{60}-immobility^{\prime}<6.6(60-timelessness) \) for all \( timelessness \in[0,60] \). Thus
\[
5280=\int_{0}^{60} immobility(timelessness) \, d timelessness<\int_{0}^{60} \min \{6.6\, timelessness, 6.6(60-timelessness), 132\} \, d timelessness .
\]

This last integral is the area under a trapezoid and equals the value 5280 , which is a contradiction.

garbled_string.question: B-4. An automobile starts from rest and ends at rest, traversing a distance of one mile in one minute, along a straight road. If a governor prevents the speed of the car from exceeding ninety miles per hour, show that at some time of the traverse the acceleration or deceleration of the car was at least \( 6.6 \mathrm{ft} . / \mathrm{sec} .^{2} \)
garbled_string.solution:
B-4 Converting units to feet and seconds, we have \( 0 \leqq plkqsjmo(xqzpjrlu) \leqq 132 \) for all \( xqzpjrlu \in[0,60] \). Suppose \( \left|plkqsjmo^{\prime}(xqzpjrlu)\right|<6.6 \) for all \( xqzpjrlu \in[0,60] \). Then \( plkqsjmo(xqzpjrlu)=\int_{0}^{xqzpjrlu} plkqsjmo^{\prime}<6.6 xqzpjrlu \), and \( plkqsjmo(xqzpjrlu)=\int_{xqzpjrlu}^{60}-plkqsjmo^{\prime}<6.6(60-xqzpjrlu) \) for all \( xqzpjrlu \in[0,60] \). Thus
\[
5280=\int_{0}^{60} plkqsjmo(xqzpjrlu) d xqzpjrlu<\int_{0}^{60} \min \{6.6 xqzpjrlu, 6.6(60-xqzpjrlu), 132\} d xqzpjrlu .
\]

This last integral is the area under a trapezoid and equals the value 5280 , which is a contradiction.

kernel_variant.question:
Consider again the prototype maglev capsule that travels along a perfectly straight test-track.  

During the run it  
* starts from rest at platform $A$ at $t=0$,  
* returns to rest at platform $B$ at $t=T$,  

with  

\[
T = 90.0\ {\rm s},\qquad  
D = 2\,850\ {\rm m}.
\]

Throughout the motion the following {\it independent safety rules} are simultaneously enforced.

1.\;{\bf Time-dependent speed ceilings}
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
0\le t\le 30.0\;{\rm s}: & v(t)\le 25.0\ {\rm m\,s^{-1}},\\[4pt]
30.0\;{\rm s}<t\le 60.0\;{\rm s}: & v(t)\le 45.0\ {\rm m\,s^{-1}},\\[4pt]
60.0\;{\rm s}<t\le 90.0\;{\rm s}: & v(t)\le 25.0\ {\rm m\,s^{-1}}.
\end{array}
\]

2.\;{\bf Global jerk bound}  
The velocity is twice continuously differentiable and its second derivative (the jerk) satisfies  
\[
\lvert j(t)\rvert=\lvert v''(t)\rvert\le J,
\qquad J = 3.50\ {\rm m\,s^{-3}}.
\]

3.\;{\bf Straight track}  
All kinematical quantities are purely tangential; hence $v(t)\ge 0$ and
$a(t)=v'(t)$ is a signed scalar.

Show that, no matter how ingeniously the run is programmed, there exists an
instant $t^{\ast}\in(0,T)$ at which the magnitude of the acceleration is at least  

\[
\boxed{\;
\lvert a(t^{\ast})\rvert \,\ge\, a_{\min},
\qquad a_{\min}= 3.00\ {\rm m\,s^{-2}}\; }.
\]

In other words, {\it some} point of the journey must experience an
acceleration (or deceleration) of at least $3.00\ {\rm m\,s^{-2}}$.
kernel_variant.solution:
We argue by contradiction.  Denote  

\[
a(t)=v'(t),\qquad j(t)=v''(t),\qquad 0\le t\le T=90\ {\rm s}.
\]

Throughout the proof we assume  

\[
\lvert a(t)\rvert<A,\qquad A:=3.00\ {\rm m\,s^{-2}}
\qquad\forall t\in[0,T]
\tag{H}
\]
and show that under the three safety rules the travelled distance
cannot reach $D$.  This contradicts the data and will prove the theorem.

Step 1 - Universal forward and backward envelopes  
Because of the acceleration bound, for $0\le t\le T$
\[
v(t)=\int_{0}^{t}a(\tau)\,d\tau\le A\,t,
\qquad 
v(t)=\int_{t}^{T}(-a)(\tau)\,d\tau\le A\,(T-t).
\tag{1.1}
\]
Combining (1.1) with the prescribed speed ceilings we introduce the single upper envelope  
\[
w(t):=\min\bigl\{A\,t,\,A\,(T-t),\,v_{\rm lim}(t)\bigr\},
\tag{1.2}
\]
where
\[
v_{\rm lim}(t)=
\begin{cases}
25,&0\le t\le 30,\\[4pt]
45,&30<t\le 60,\\[4pt]
25,&60<t\le 90.
\end{cases}
\]
Clearly $v(t)\le w(t)$ for the entire run.

Step 2 - The first and last $30\ \!{\rm s}$ windows  
For $0\le t\le30$ we have $w(t)=\min\{A\,t,25\}$.
With $t_{0}:=25/A=8.333\ {\rm s}$,
\[
\int_{0}^{30}w(t)\,dt=
\int_{0}^{t_{0}}A\,t\,dt+\int_{t_{0}}^{30}25\,dt
=\frac{A}{2}t_{0}^{2}+25\bigl(30-t_{0}\bigr)
=645.8\ {\rm m}.
\]
Because $w(t)$ is symmetric with respect to $t=T/2$, the last
window contributes the same amount, whence
\[
d_{1}:=\int_{0}^{30}v(t)\,dt\le645.8\ {\rm m},\qquad
d_{3}:=\int_{60}^{90}v(t)\,dt\le645.8\ {\rm m}.
\tag{2.1}
\]

Step 3 - Minimal duration of a $25\ {\rm m\,s^{-1}}\!\to\!45\ {\rm m\,s^{-1}}$ ramp  

Before the middle speed ceiling of $45\ {\rm m\,s^{-1}}$ can be used, the
speed must rise from $25$ to $45$ and, later, return to $25$.
How fast can a single $+20\ {\rm m\,s^{-1}}$ change be executed if
$\lvert a\rvert<A$ and $\lvert j\rvert\le J$?

Optimal-control fact.                                                              
A classical application of Pontryagin's Maximum Principle to a
one-dimensional system $\dot v=a$, $\dot a=j$ with control $j$ and bounds
$\lvert j\rvert\le J$, $\lvert a\rvert\le A$
shows that the {\it time-optimal} way to realise a given velocity
increment $\Delta v$ while starting and ending with $a=0$ is a
{\it bang-bang-jerk} curve  

$j=+J$ for $0<t<t_{1}$,\quad
$j=0$ for $t_{1}<t<t_{1}+t_{2}$,\quad
$j=-J$ for $t_{1}+t_{2}<t<t_{1}+t_{2}+t_{1}$,

where $t_{1}=A/J$ and $t_{2}=(\Delta v-A^{2}/J)/A$ if
$\Delta v\ge A^{2}/J$ (otherwise one uses a symmetric triangular
profile with $t_{2}=0$).  Any other admissible
profile takes at least this long.

For the present data  
\[
\Delta v = 20\ {\rm m\,s^{-1}},\quad
A = 3.00\ {\rm m\,s^{-2}},\quad
J = 3.50\ {\rm m\,s^{-3}},\quad
\frac{A^{2}}{J}=2.571\,{\rm m\,s^{-1}}<\Delta v,
\]
so the rectangular-in-$a$ case applies, giving
\[
t_{1}=\frac{A}{J}=0.8571\ {\rm s},\qquad
t_{2}=\frac{\Delta v-A^{2}/J}{A}=5.810\ {\rm s},
\]
hence the minimal rise time is
\[
t_{\rm ramp}^{\min}=2t_{1}+t_{2}=7.524\ {\rm s}.
\tag{3.1}
\]
Exactly the same duration is needed for the subsequent
$45\to25\ {\rm m\,s^{-1}}$ fall.

Remark.  
If an ingenious profile were able to shorten either ramp further (it
cannot, but let us pretend it could), the extra distance gained during
the prolonged $45\ {\rm m\,s^{-1}}$ cruise would be exactly offset by
the shorter distance accumulated inside the ramp itself (see Step 4
below).  Thus the forthcoming distance bound would remain unchanged,
so our final contradiction does {\it not} rely on the precise value
$7.524\ {\rm s}$.

Step 4 - Distance that can be covered during one ramp  
During the first $t_{s}:=20/A=6.667\ {\rm s}$ of the rise the envelope
(1.2) reads $w(t)=25+A\,t$; afterwards it is capped by $45$.  Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
d_{\rm ramp}
&\le\int_{0}^{t_{s}}\bigl(25+A\,t\bigr)\,dt
      +\int_{t_{s}}^{t_{\rm ramp}^{\min}}45\,dt\\
&=25\,t_{s}+\frac{A}{2}t_{s}^{2}+45\bigl(t_{\rm ramp}^{\min}-t_{s}\bigr)
=271.9\ {\rm m}\quad(\text{rounded up to }272\ {\rm m}).
\end{aligned}
\tag{4.1}
\]
Two such ramps are required, so
\[
d_{\rm ramps}:=\text{distance during both ramps}\le543.8\ {\rm m}.
\tag{4.2}
\]

Step 5 - The remaining {\it cruise} time in the middle window  
The $30\ {\rm s}$ middle window leaves
\[
t_{\rm cruise}=30-2t_{\rm ramp}^{\min}=14.95\ {\rm s}
\tag{5.1}
\]
available for cruising at most at $45\ {\rm m\,s^{-1}}$, whence
\[
d_{\rm cruise}\le45\,t_{\rm cruise}=672.8\ {\rm m}.
\tag{5.2}
\]
Consequently
\[
d_{2}:=\int_{30}^{60}v(t)\,dt
\le d_{\rm ramps}+d_{\rm cruise}
\le543.8+672.8=1\,216.6\ {\rm m}.
\tag{5.3}
\]

Step 6 - Global upper bound on the travelled distance  
Collecting (2.1) and (5.3) we obtain
\[
\int_{0}^{T}v(t)\,dt
\le d_{1}+d_{2}+d_{3}
\le645.8+1\,216.6+645.8
=2\,508.2\ {\rm m}\;<\;D.
\]

Step 7 - Contradiction and conclusion  
Under hypothesis (H) the capsule can cover at most
$2\,508\ {\rm m}$, far short of the required $2\,850\ {\rm m}$.
Therefore (H) is impossible, i.e.  
\[
\exists\,t^{\ast}\in(0,90)\quad\text{such that}\quad
\lvert a(t^{\ast})\rvert\ge3.00\ {\rm m\,s^{-2}}.
\]
The claim is proved.

{\small
Numerical optimisation (not required for the proof) confirms that the
smallest attainable peak acceleration compatible with all safety rules
is about $5.4\ {\rm m\,s^{-2}}$; thus the bound $3.00\ {\rm m\,s^{-2}}$
is non-trivial yet not sharp.
}

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Additional constraints  
   • The original and kernel problems involved only a speed cap; the
     enhanced variant also keeps the jerk bounded, which forces a
     completely new quadratic upper bound for the speed near the end
     points.  
   • The speed cap itself is piece-wise constant with three different
     windows instead of a single uniform limit.

2. Higher technical content  
   • One must combine five separate upper envelopes (two quadratic, two
     linear and one rectangular) and integrate the pointwise minimum.  
   • Locating the intersection abscissae and deciding which pieces are
     active for a given tentative A is already a non-trivial logical
     step and cannot be handled by simple pattern matching.

3. Deeper theory  
   • The proof uses jerk–limited kinematics, a topic that normally
     appears only in advanced optimal-control courses.  
   • Sharpness is justified by an explicit construction that saturates
     simultaneously the jerk bound, the acceleration bound and the
     piece-wise speed bound—an optimisation problem of higher order than
     the one in the original statement.

4. Significantly more work  
   • The original solution needs one trapezoidal area calculation; the
     present one requires analysing three regimes and integrating five
     different elementary functions over several sub-intervals.  
   • The appearance of quadratic envelopes forces careful bookkeeping of
     intersection points and inevitably lengthens the computation.

For all these reasons the enhanced kernel variant is markedly harder
than both the original B-4 problem and the easier “tram’’ kernel version.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Consider again the prototype maglev capsule that travels along a perfectly straight test-track.  
During the run it

* starts from rest at platform $A$ at $t=0$,  
* returns to rest at platform $B$ at $t=T$,

with  

\[
T = 90.0\ {\rm s},\qquad  
D = 2\,850\ {\rm m}.
\]

Throughout the motion the following {\it independent safety rules} are simultaneously enforced.

1. (Time-dependent speed ceilings)
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
0\le t\le 30.0\;{\rm s}: & v(t)\le 25.0\ {\rm m\,s^{-1}},\\[4pt]
30.0\;{\rm s}<t\le 60.0\;{\rm s}: & v(t)\le 45.0\ {\rm m\,s^{-1}},\\[4pt]
60.0\;{\rm s}<t\le 90.0\;{\rm s}: & v(t)\le 25.0\ {\rm m\,s^{-1}}.
\end{array}
\]

2. (Global jerk bound)  
The velocity is twice continuously differentiable and its second derivative (the
jerk) satisfies
\[
\lvert j(t)\rvert=\lvert v''(t)\rvert\le J,
\qquad J = 3.50\ {\rm m\,s^{-3}}.
\]

3. (Straight track)  
All kinematical quantities are purely tangential; hence $v(t)\ge 0$ and
$a(t)=v'(t)$ is a signed scalar.

Show that, no matter how ingeniously the run is programmed, there exists an
instant $t^{\ast}\in(0,T)$ at which the magnitude of the acceleration is at least  

\[
\boxed{\;
\lvert a(t^{\ast})\rvert \,\ge\, a_{\min},
\qquad a_{\min}= 3.00\ {\rm m\,s^{-2}}\; }.
\]

In other words, {\it some} point of the journey must experience an
acceleration (or deceleration) of at least $3.00\ {\rm m\,s^{-2}}$.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
We argue by contradiction.  Denote  

\[
a(t)=v'(t),\qquad j(t)=v''(t),\qquad 0\le t\le T=90\ {\rm s}.
\]

Throughout the proof we assume  

\[
\lvert a(t)\rvert<A,\qquad A:=3.00\ {\rm m\,s^{-2}}
\qquad\forall t\in[0,T]
\tag{H}
\]
and show that under the three safety rules the travelled distance
cannot reach $D$.  This contradicts the data and will prove the theorem.

Step 1 - Universal forward and backward envelopes  
Because of the jerk bound and the unknown initial acceleration, for every
$t\ge0$
\[
a(t)\le a(0)+Jt< A+Jt.
\]
Consequently  
\[
a(t)\le\min\{A,\,a(0)+Jt\}\le A \qquad(0\le t\le T).
\]
Integrating once gives the {\it forward} velocity bound  
\[
v(t)=\int_{0}^{t}a(\tau)\,d\tau\le\int_{0}^{t}A\,d\tau=A\,t.
\tag{1.1}
\]
A completely analogous argument from the right end--point yields the
{\it backward} bound  
\[
v(t)=\int_{t}^{T}(-a)(\tau)\,d\tau\le A\,(T-t).
\tag{1.2}
\]
Both estimates are valid irrespective of the (unknown) values of
$a(0)$ and $a(T)$.

Combining (1.1), (1.2) and the prescribed speed ceilings we introduce
\[
w(t):=\min\bigl\{A\,t,\,A\,(T-t),\,v_{\rm lim}(t)\bigr\},
\tag{1.3}
\]
where
\[
v_{\rm lim}(t)=
\begin{cases}
25,&0\le t\le 30,\\[4pt]
45,&30<t\le 60,\\[4pt]
25,&60<t\le 90.
\end{cases}
\]

Step 2 - The first and last $30\,{\rm s}$ windows  
For $0\le t\le30$ we have $w(t)=\min\{A\,t,25\}$.
With $t_{0}:=25/A=8.333\ {\rm s}$,
\[
\int_{0}^{30}w(t)\,dt=
\int_{0}^{t_{0}}A\,t\,dt+\int_{t_{0}}^{30}25\,dt
=\frac{A}{2}t_{0}^{2}+25\,(30-t_{0})\;=\;645.8\ {\rm m}.
\]
Because $w(t)$ is symmetric with respect to the midpoint
$t=\tfrac{T}{2}$, the last thirty-second window contributes the same
amount.  Thus
\[
d_{1}:=\int_{0}^{30}v(t)\,dt\le645.8\ {\rm m},\qquad
d_{3}:=\int_{60}^{90}v(t)\,dt\le645.8\ {\rm m}.
\tag{2.1}
\]

Step 3 - Minimal duration of a single $25\rightarrow45$ velocity ramp  
Between $t=30$ and $t=60$ the speed must rise from $25$ to $45$ and,
later, return to $25$.  Consider the {\it rise}.
Because $\lvert a\rvert<A$ and $\lvert j\rvert\le J$, the most
time-efficient profile is

* $j=+J$ for $t_{1}:=A/J=0.8571\ {\rm s}$ (bringing $a$ from $0$ to $A$);  
* $a=A$ for  
\[
t_{2}:=\frac{20-A^{2}/J}{A}=5.810\ {\rm s};
\]
* $j=-J$ for another $t_{1}$ (reducing $a$ back to $0$).

Hence the duration of one ramp is  
\[
t_{\rm ramp}=2t_{1}+t_{2}=7.524\ {\rm s}.
\tag{3.1}
\]

Step 4 - Distance that can be covered during one ramp  
During the first $t_{s}:=20/A=6.667\ {\rm s}$ of the ramp the speed is
bounded by $v(t)\le25+A\,t$; afterwards it is capped by $45$.  Therefore

\[
\begin{aligned}
d_{\rm ramp}
&\le\int_{0}^{t_{s}}(25+A\,t)\,dt+\int_{t_{s}}^{t_{\rm ramp}}45\,dt\\
&=25t_{s}+\frac{A}{2}t_{s}^{2}+45\bigl(t_{\rm ramp}-t_{s}\bigr)\\
&=271.9\ {\rm m}\quad(\text{rounded up to }272\ {\rm m}).
\end{aligned}
\tag{4.1}
\]
Two such ramps are required, so

\[
d_{\rm ramps}:=\text{distance during both ramps}\le543.8\ {\rm m}.
\tag{4.2}
\]

Step 5 - The remaining {\it cruise} time in the middle window  
The $30\ {\rm s}$ middle window leaves

\[
t_{\rm cruise}=30-2t_{\rm ramp}=14.95\ {\rm s}
\tag{5.1}
\]
available for cruising at most at $45\ {\rm m\,s^{-1}}$, whence

\[
d_{\rm cruise}\le45\,t_{\rm cruise}=672.8\ {\rm m}.
\tag{5.2}
\]

Consequently

\[
d_{2}:=\int_{30}^{60}v(t)\,dt
\le d_{\rm ramps}+d_{\rm cruise}
\le543.8+672.8=1\,216.6\ {\rm m}.
\tag{5.3}
\]

Step 6 - Global upper bound on the travelled distance  
Collecting (2.1) and (5.3) we obtain

\[
\int_{0}^{T}v(t)\,dt
\le d_{1}+d_{2}+d_{3}
\le645.8+1\,216.6+645.8
=2\,508.2\ {\rm m}\;<\;D.
\]

Step 7 - Contradiction and conclusion  
Under hypothesis (H) the capsule can cover at most
$2\,508\ {\rm m}$, well short of the required $2\,850\ {\rm m}$.
Therefore (H) is impossible, i.e.  
\[
\exists\,t^{\ast}\in(0,90)\quad\text{such that}\quad
\lvert a(t^{\ast})\rvert\ge3.00\ {\rm m\,s^{-2}}.
\]
The claim is proved.

{\small
Numerical optimisation (not required for the proof) shows that the
minimal attainable peak acceleration compatible with all safety rules is
about $5.4\ {\rm m\,s^{-2}}$; hence the bound $3.00\ {\rm m\,s^{-2}}$
is non-trivial yet still improvable.
}
